overview: Win on card quality and flexibility. Trade one-for-one with aggro until their threats run out, then pull ahead with resilient, high-value cards; against control, become the aggressor with hard-to-answer threats. Adapt your role to the opponent.
win_conditions: Grind the opponent out of resources, then close with efficient threats
Resolve a powerful must-answer permanent the opponent cannot deal with

keep_criteria: Three or four lands with a mix of interaction and threats
A functional curve and at least one card-advantage or high-value card
Enough flexibility to play either the beatdown or the control role
mulligan_criteria: Two or fewer lands with mostly expensive cards
No interaction at all against an unknown field
A hand that cannot meaningfully act before turn 4

examples.reason: Lands, a removal spell, and two solid threats can adapt to any opponent.
Two lands and four cards that all cost four-plus mana will fall hopelessly behind.

early_game: Use removal on the opponent's most dangerous early threats
Develop lands and hold up interaction; do not waste answers on filler
Decide early whether you are the beatdown or the control deck this game
mid_game: Deploy your highest-value threats and force the opponent to have answers
Trade efficiently — every clean one-for-one favors the better deck
Keep a card-advantage engine or threat in reserve against sweepers
late_game: Win the long game on card quality; rebuild faster than the opponent
Close once ahead — do not give a stabilizing opponent free turns

key_cards.name: Efficient removal
key_cards.notes: Spend it on threats that actually pressure you, not on every creature.

key_cards.name: Value threats
key_cards.notes: Creatures and planeswalkers that generate card advantage as they survive.

key_cards.name: Card advantage
key_cards.notes: Refills that let you out-grind the opponent in the long game.

sequencing_tips: Identify your role each game and sequence accordingly
Hold threats around suspected board wipes; do not overcommit
Use removal reactively unless proactively removing it wins the race

matchups.advice: Be the control deck: trade, stabilize the board, then win with card quality.
Be the aggressor: deploy threats and force answers before they stabilize.

common_threats: Faster aggro decks that kill before card quality matters
Combo decks that ignore your board entirely
Resource engines that out-grind your value plan
